name: Trident of Shiva
description: The Trishula, or Trident of Shiva, is one of the most potent and recognizable symbols in Hindu mythology, representing the three fundamental aspects of Shiva: creation, preservation, and destruction. It is not merely a weapon, but a representation of the cosmic forces that govern the universe and the balance between them. The three prongs are often interpreted as representing the three gunas (qualities of nature): Sattva (goodness, purity), Rajas (passion, activity), and Tamas (ignorance, inertia). Shiva wields the Trishula to maintain cosmic order, destroy evil, and liberate souls from the cycle of rebirth. It is said to have been created by Vishwakarma, the divine architect, using the sun's energy. The Trident's power is immense, capable of obliterating mountains and drying up oceans. Symbolically, it also represents Shiva's control over the physical, mental, and spiritual realms. Depictions often show Shiva holding the Trishula during his Tandava dance, a dance of cosmic creation and destruction. The Trishula is not just a weapon of destruction, but also a symbol of protection and spiritual power, reminding devotees of Shiva's ability to overcome all obstacles and grant liberation. It is a powerful emblem of divine sovereignty and the ultimate reality beyond duality.
subtitle: Symbol of power, creation, and destruction
origin: Forged by Vishwakarma from the sun's energy
currentLocation: Held by Shiva, its exact physical location is not specified

keyMyths.title: Creation of the Trishula
keyMyths.description: Vishwakarma, the divine architect, forged the Trishula from the sun's energy for Shiva. It became his signature weapon and symbol.

keyMyths.title: Trishula and Destruction
keyMyths.description: Shiva uses the Trishula to destroy evil and maintain cosmic balance. It represents the destructive aspect necessary for renewal.

sources.author: Wendy Doniger
sources.description: Analysis of Shiva's symbolism and mythology.
sources.text: Analysis of Shiva's symbolism and mythology.

sources.author: Alain Danielou
